title: Cotula sessilis
intro: Cotula sessilis, also known as Buttonweed or Button Marshwort, is a low-growing, perennial herb native to Australia and New Zealand. The health benefits of Cotula sessilis include its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in wound healing, reducing fever, and treating infections. Bioactive constituents of Cotula sessilis include sesquiterpenes, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Cotula sessilis include teas, tinctures, and infusions, which are traditionally used to treat colds, flu, and skin conditions in Australian and New Zealand folk medicine.
taxonomy: Cotula sessilis is classified within the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. It belongs to the genus Cotula, which is a group of small, annual or perennial herbs native to Australia. The species is further classified as a member of the tribe Inuleae, within the subfamily Asteroideae. Cotula sessilis is native to Australia, where it can be found growing in wetland and aquatic environments. This classification reflects the plant's close relationships with other members of the daisy family.
uses: Cotula sessilis, commonly known as straggly buttonweed or buttonweed, has been used in traditional medicine for its purported health benefits. The plant is said to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may be useful in treating various conditions. In some parts of the world, Cotula sessilis is used to treat digestive issues, skin problems, and respiratory infections. However, more research is needed to confirm the effectiveness and safety of using Cotula sessilis as a medicinal herb. Its use should be approached with caution and under the guidance of a qualified healthcare professional.

properties: Cotula sessilis, commonly known as buttonweed, is believed to have various therapeutic properties. It is traditionally used in folk medicine to treat respiratory issues, such as coughs and bronchitis. The plant's leaves and flowers are rich in antioxidants, which may help to reduce inflammation and combat oxidative stress. In some cultures, Cotula sessilis is also used to treat skin conditions, such as eczema and acne, due to its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. Further research is needed to confirm the effectiveness of these uses, but the plant has been valued for its medicinal properties for centuries.

preparations: Cotula sessilis is also known as Australian bog-rice-flower or water button.
